content.text: Environment Config and Secrets Separation

Configuration and secrets are two distinct categories that get conflated in practice, leading to security incidents and environment drift. Configuration is any value that shapes how the application behaves — timeouts, model versions, log levels, feature flags. A secret is a credential that grants access to a resource — API keys, database passwords, signing keys.

GenAI applications have a larger configuration surface than conventional services: model endpoint URLs, API versions, prompt file digests, temperature settings, retrieval index locations, eval thresholds. Getting the separation right means each value lives in exactly one place, rotates automatically, and is scoped to the minimum blast radius.

attrs.alt: Three-tier config model and secret lifecycle for GenAI pipelines showing baked, env-specific, and secret tiers with anti-patterns

content.text: The Three-Tier Configuration Model
Every configuration value belongs in exactly one tier based on how it changes and who can read it:
Tier 1 — Baked into the image.

Values that must be identical for the artifact to be reproducible: model API version, prompt file content hash, app version string. These travel with the Docker image and are never overridden at runtime. Changing them requires a new build.
Tier 2 — Environment-specific config (non-secret).

Values that differ per environment but contain no credentials: log level, replica count, timeout values, retrieval index URL, feature flags. Stored in Kubernetes ConfigMaps or SSM Parameter Store (standard parameters). Readable by anyone with cluster access.
Tier 3 — Secrets.

Credentials that grant access to external systems: model API keys, vector database passwords, webhook signing secrets, OAuth tokens. Stored in AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault. Injected at pod startup via ExternalSecret controller. Never present in image layers, ConfigMaps, or application logs.
Tier 1: Baking Configuration Into the Image

FROM python:3.12-slim

# Build-time arguments — injected by CI, never from .env files
ARG APP_VERSION
ARG MODEL_API_VERSION
ARG BUILD_TIMESTAMP
ARG GIT_SHA

# Bake as image labels (readable via docker inspect / /version endpoint)
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.version="${APP_VERSION}" \
      com.myco.model-api-version="${MODEL_API_VERSION}" \
      com.myco.git-sha="${GIT_SHA}" \
      com.myco.built-at="${BUILD_TIMESTAMP}"

# Bake as env vars readable by the application at runtime
ENV APP_VERSION=${APP_VERSION} \
    MODEL_API_VERSION=${MODEL_API_VERSION} \
    BUILD_TIMESTAMP=${BUILD_TIMESTAMP}

# Copy prompt files and compute their hash into the image
COPY prompts/ /app/prompts/
RUN sha256sum /app/prompts/*.txt | sort | sha256sum | cut -d' ' -f1 \
    > /app/prompts/.digest

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY app/ /app/
CMD ["uvicorn", "app.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8080"]
The prompt digest file at
/app/prompts/.digest
is computed from the actual prompt content. The
/version
endpoint reads this file and reports it, so the parity checker can verify that all environments are running the same prompt version.
Tier 2: Environment Config via ConfigMaps and Helm
Non-secret environment values are managed through Helm values files — one per environment, checked into the repository:

# helm/values-production.yaml
app:
  logLevel: info
  replicaCount: 5
  timeoutSeconds: 30
  retrieval:
    indexUrl: "https://prod-retrieval.internal/v1"
    topK: 5
  model:
    # Note: endpoint URL here; API key is in Secrets Manager, not here
    endpoint: "https://api.openai.com/v1"
    maxRetries: 3
    requestTimeoutSeconds: 25
  featureFlags:
    streamingEnabled: true
    experimentalRanking: false
  eval:
    accuracyThreshold: 0.95
    safetyThreshold: 1.0

# helm/values-staging.yaml  (intentional differences documented)
app:
  logLevel: debug          # more verbose in staging
  replicaCount: 2
  timeoutSeconds: 30       # same — timeout behavior must match prod
  retrieval:
    indexUrl: "https://staging-retrieval.internal/v1"
    topK: 5                # same
  model:
    endpoint: "https://api.openai.com/v1"   # same — identical API
    maxRetries: 3
    requestTimeoutSeconds: 25
  featureFlags:
    streamingEnabled: true
    experimentalRanking: true    # can test experimental features in staging
  eval:
    accuracyThreshold: 0.90      # lower threshold; staging = investigation
    safetyThreshold: 1.0         # same — safety never relaxed
Tier 3: Secrets via External Secret Operator

The External Secrets Operator syncs secrets from AWS Secrets Manager into Kubernetes Secrets, then mounts them as environment variables. The application never fetches secrets directly from Secrets Manager — it reads
os.environ
. The operator handles refresh when secrets rotate:

# k8s/external-secret-production.yaml
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: genai-app-secrets
  namespace: production
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h           # re-syncs every hour; catches rotations
  secretStoreRef:
    name: aws-secrets-manager
    kind: SecretStore
  target:
    name: genai-app-secrets     # creates this K8s Secret
    creationPolicy: Owner
  data:
    - secretKey: OPENAI_API_KEY          # env var name in pod
      remoteRef:
        key: production/genai/openai     # path in Secrets Manager
        property: api_key

    - secretKey: VECTOR_DB_PASSWORD
      remoteRef:
        key: production/genai/vectordb
        property: password

    - secretKey: WEBHOOK_SIGNING_SECRET
      remoteRef:
        key: production/genai/webhook
        property: signing_secret

# helm/templates/deployment.yaml (excerpt)
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: genai-app
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: genai-app-config    # Tier 2: non-secret config
          env:
            - name: OPENAI_API_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: genai-app-secrets  # Tier 3: injected secret
                  key: OPENAI_API_KEY
            - name: VECTOR_DB_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: genai-app-secrets
                  key: VECTOR_DB_PASSWORD
Reading Config Safely in Application Code
A single config loader validates all required values at startup, fails fast on missing secrets, and never logs secret values:

# app/config.py
from __future__ import annotations
import os
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from functools import cached_property

class ConfigError(RuntimeError):
    pass

def _require(key: str) -> str:
    """Fetch required env var; raise on missing so the pod fails fast at startup."""
    value = os.environ.get(key)
    if not value:
        raise ConfigError(f"Required environment variable '{key}' is not set")
    return value

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class AppConfig:
    # Tier 1: baked into image (read from env set in Dockerfile)
    app_version: str = field(default_factory=lambda: os.environ.get("APP_VERSION", "dev"))
    model_api_version: str = field(default_factory=lambda: os.environ.get("MODEL_API_VERSION", "gpt-4o-2024-08-06"))

    # Tier 2: environment-specific non-secret config
    log_level: str = field(default_factory=lambda: os.environ.get("LOG_LEVEL", "info"))
    timeout_seconds: int = field(default_factory=lambda: int(os.environ.get("TIMEOUT_SECONDS", "30")))
    retrieval_index_url: str = field(default_factory=lambda: _require("RETRIEVAL_INDEX_URL"))
    top_k: int = field(default_factory=lambda: int(os.environ.get("RETRIEVAL_TOP_K", "5")))

    # Tier 3: secrets (required; missing = startup failure)
    openai_api_key: str = field(default_factory=lambda: _require("OPENAI_API_KEY"))
    vector_db_password: str = field(default_factory=lambda: _require("VECTOR_DB_PASSWORD"))

    def safe_repr(self) -> dict:
        """Return config dict safe to log — secrets redacted."""
        return {
            "app_version": self.app_version,
            "model_api_version": self.model_api_version,
            "log_level": self.log_level,
            "timeout_seconds": self.timeout_seconds,
            "retrieval_index_url": self.retrieval_index_url,
            "openai_api_key": "[REDACTED]",       # never log the actual value
            "vector_db_password": "[REDACTED]",
        }

# Singleton — validated once at import time
config = AppConfig()
Always provide a
safe_repr()
method that redacts secret fields. Log this dict at startup — it gives ops teams a clear view of what config the running instance has loaded without exposing credentials.
Automatic Secret Rotation
Static API keys are a liability — a single leak is a permanent compromise until manually revoked. Automate rotation with a 90-day schedule:

# scripts/rotate_api_key.py
"""
Rotates the OpenAI API key in Secrets Manager.
Run via EventBridge cron every 90 days.
Zero-downtime: writes new key first, old key stays valid during overlap.
"""
import boto3, openai, os, json

def rotate_key(secret_name: str, overlap_seconds: int = 300) -> None:
    sm = boto3.client("secretsmanager")
    openai_admin = openai.OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_ADMIN_KEY"])

    # 1. Create new API key via OpenAI admin API
    new_key = openai_admin.api_keys.create(name=f"genai-prod-rotated")
    print(f"Created new key: {new_key.id}")

    # 2. Write new key to Secrets Manager (existing ExternalSecret syncs within 1h,
    #    or force a reconcile via annotation update)
    current = json.loads(sm.get_secret_value(SecretId=secret_name)["SecretString"])
    current["api_key"] = new_key.plaintext_key
    current["rotated_at"] = __import__("datetime").datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
    sm.put_secret_value(SecretId=secret_name, SecretString=json.dumps(current))
    print(f"Updated Secrets Manager: {secret_name}")

    # 3. Wait for ExternalSecret to sync (or trigger reconcile)
    import time; time.sleep(overlap_seconds)

    # 4. Delete old key (it was stored in the previous secret version)
    previous = sm.get_secret_value(SecretId=secret_name, VersionStage="AWSPREVIOUS")
    old_key_id = json.loads(previous["SecretString"]).get("key_id")
    if old_key_id:
        openai_admin.api_keys.delete(old_key_id)
        print(f"Deleted old key: {old_key_id}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    rotate_key("production/genai/openai")
Pre-commit Secret Scanning
The last line of defense before a secret reaches the remote is a pre-commit hook that scans staged changes for credential patterns:

# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks
    rev: v8.18.0
    hooks:
      - id: gitleaks
        name: Detect hardcoded secrets
        # Blocks commit if any secret pattern is found in staged diff

  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v4.5.0
    hooks:
      - id: detect-private-key      # catches PEM private keys
      - id: check-added-large-files  # catches accidentally committed credential dumps

# .gitleaks.toml — add allowlist for false positives (test fixtures, examples)
[allowlist]
  description = "Allowlisted test fixtures"
  paths = [
    "tests/fixtures/",
    "docs/examples/",
  ]
  regexes = [
    "EXAMPLE_API_KEY",          # used in docs as placeholder
    "sk-test-.*",               # OpenAI test keys (non-functional)
  ]
Summary
Configuration has three tiers: baked into the image (immutable per build), environment-specific non-secret values (ConfigMap/Helm), and secrets (Secrets Manager/Vault injected at runtime).

Secrets never appear in Dockerfile layers, git history, ConfigMaps, CLI arguments, or application logs — they live only in the secret store and are injected as environment variables by the External Secrets Operator.
Each environment has its own scoped secret values; dev and prod never share credentials so a dev compromise cannot grant prod access.
A startup config loader validates all required values on pod init and fails fast on missing secrets, surfacing configuration errors before traffic reaches the pod.
Pre-commit secret scanning and automated 90-day rotation close the two most common leak vectors: accidental commits and stale long-lived credentials.
